---
title: 'Final Project: Can Emotional Intelligence Predict Academic Performance'
Date: 12/1/2024
Group_Members: K. Banu, J. Kawal, K.  Shanmugasundaram, and A. Zuger
output:
  html_document: default
  pdf_document: default
  word_document: default
Professor: Dr. Jin Fang
Course: STAT 4600
---

```{r}
# Title: Can EI be used as an alternative measure of potential student success
# when evaluating admissions for graduate students?
# Date: 12/1/2024
# Group Members: K. Banu, J. Kawal, K.  Shanmugasundaram, and A. Zuger
# Course: STAT 4600
# Professor: Dr. Jin Fang

# Attestation Statement
# The code provided was taken from the following sources: 

# Grolemund, Garrett, and Hadley Wickham. R for Data Science. O’Reilly Media, 2017.​

# Long, JD, and Paul Teetor. R Cookbook. 2nd ed., O'Reilly Media, 2019. ISBN: 978-1-492-04068-2.​

# Almog, Uri. “Decision Trees, Explained.” Medium, 2 June 2022, https://towardsdatascience.com/decision-trees-explained-d7678c43a59e.​

# “Decision Tree for Regression in R Programming.” GeeksforGeeks, 26 July 2020, https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/decision-tree-for-regression-in-r-programming/.​

# https://topepo.github.io/caret/variable-importance.html 

# https://www.datacamp.com/tutorial/decision-trees-R

# https://christophm.github.io/interpretable-ml-book/pdp.html

# https://journal.r-project.org/archive/2017/RJ-2017-016/RJ-2017-016.pdf

# https://www.science.smith.edu/~jcrouser/SDS293/labs/lab14-r.html

# https://stackoverflow.com/questions/39886562/how-to-check-interaction-effects-for-a-lot-of-predictors-in-r

# https://stats.stackexchange.com/questions/278800/predict-using-trained-model-on-dataset

# https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/cross-validation-in-r-programming/

# OpenAI. (2024). ChatGPT (November 2024 version) [Large language model]. Retrieved from https://chat.openai.com/ 

# AI was used solely for debugging.

```

```{r}
# Part 1: View Data, Trim Data, Calculate Descriptive Statistics.

# Step 1: Downloading Data Package. 

# Data upload and visualization

#install.packages("psymetadata")
#library(psymetadata)

#data("maccann2020")

#setwd("C:/Users/angel/OneDrive/Desktop/Final_code_files")

#write.csv(maccann2020, "maccann2020.csv", row.names = FALSE)


# Note: excluded code after running and downloading the csv file. Could not stitch to html with code present
```



```{r}
# Start here: MccCan 2020 CSV is located in the accompanying folder
maccann2020 <- read.csv("C:\\Users\\angel\\OneDrive\\Desktop\\Final_code_files\\maccann2020.csv")


summary(maccann2020)

# Step 2: Install packages.
# Install Packages

#Manual CRAN mirror
options(repos = c(CRAN = "https://cloud.r-project.org/"))

# Fix for html 
unlink("C:/Users/angel/AppData/Local/R/win-library/4.4/00LOCK", recursive = TRUE)
install.packages("rpart")


library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(corrplot)
library(tidyr)
library(kableExtra)
library(formattable)
library(rlang)

# Important Data Before Trim
# Publication dates:1997-2019
# Age of respondents: Min 7,Max 40, mean = 18.76
# countries represented: Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, USA, Nigeria, and Italy
# respondents: 77% white
# respondents: 62% female

# Step 3: Read McCann Data into a data frame.

EI_AP_Data <- data.frame(maccann2020)

# Descriptive Statistics

head(EI_AP_Data)

str(EI_AP_Data)

summary(EI_AP_Data) 

# Variance and standard error calculated for quantitative variables

var(EI_AP_Data$pub_year, na.rm = TRUE)
sd(EI_AP_Data$pub_year, na.rm = TRUE)

var(EI_AP_Data$yi, na.rm = TRUE)
sd(EI_AP_Data$yi, na.rm = TRUE)

var(EI_AP_Data$vi, na.rm = TRUE)
sd(EI_AP_Data$vi, na.rm = TRUE)

var(EI_AP_Data$n, na.rm = TRUE)
sd(EI_AP_Data$n, na.rm = TRUE)

var(EI_AP_Data$perc_white, na.rm = TRUE)
sd(EI_AP_Data$perc_white, na.rm = TRUE)

var(EI_AP_Data$age, na.rm = TRUE)
sd(EI_AP_Data$age, na.rm = TRUE)

var(EI_AP_Data$perc_female, na.rm = TRUE)
sd(EI_AP_Data$perc_female, na.rm = TRUE)

# Visualizations of Descriptive Statistics


# Visualization of outliers for all variables in the `vars` list using boxplots
# Assuming you have a list of variable names in the `vars` list
vars <- c("pub_year", "yi", "vi", "n", "perc_white", "age", "perc_female")

# Check for non-finite values in each variable
summary(EI_AP_Data[, vars])

# Alternatively, check the specific variables for NAs
sapply(EI_AP_Data[, vars], function(x) sum(is.na(x)))
for (var in vars) {
  p <- ggplot(EI_AP_Data, aes(x = 1, y = !!sym(var))) + 
    geom_boxplot(outlier.colour = "red", outlier.size = 3) +
    labs(title = paste("Boxplot of", var), y = var) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(axis.text.x = element_blank(), axis.ticks.x = element_blank())
  
  # Print the plot
  print(p)
}

# Proportion tables Created for Categorical Variables


# Country
length(EI_AP_Data$country)
sum(is.na(EI_AP_Data$country))
country_data_o <- EI_AP_Data$country[!is.na(EI_AP_Data$country)]
prop_table_o1 <- prop.table(table(country_data_o)) * 100
prop_table_o1 <- round(prop_table_o1, 2)
prop_df1 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_o1), Percentage = prop_table_o1)
print(prop_df1)


# Publication Type
length(EI_AP_Data$pub_type)
sum(is.na(EI_AP_Data$pub_type))
pub_type_data_o <- EI_AP_Data$pub_type[!is.na(EI_AP_Data$pub_type)]
prop_table_o2 <- prop.table(table(pub_type_data_o)) * 100
prop_table_o2 <- round(prop_table_o2, 2)
prop_df2 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_o2), Percentage = prop_table_o2)
print(prop_df2)

# Publication Year
pub_year_data_o <- EI_AP_Data$pub_year[!is.na(EI_AP_Data$pub_year)]
prop_table_o3 <- prop.table(table(pub_year_data_o)) * 100
prop_table_o3 <- round(prop_table_o3, 2)
prop_df3 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_o3), Percentage = prop_table_o3)
print(prop_df3)

#Education Level 1
length(EI_AP_Data$ed_level1)
sum(is.na(EI_AP_Data$ed_level1))
ed_level1_data_o <- EI_AP_Data$ed_level1[!is.na(EI_AP_Data$ed_level1)]
prop_table_o4 <- prop.table(table(ed_level1_data_o)) * 100
prop_table_o4 <- round(prop_table_o4, 2)
prop_df4 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_o4), Percentage = prop_table_o4)
print(prop_df4)

# EI Stream
length(EI_AP_Data$ei_stream)
sum(is.na(EI_AP_Data$ei_stream))
ei_stream_data_o <- EI_AP_Data$ei_stream[!is.na(EI_AP_Data$ei_stream)]
prop_table_o5 <- prop.table(table(ei_stream_data_o)) * 100
prop_table_o5 <- round(prop_table_o5, 2)
prop_df5 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_o5), Percentage = prop_table_o5)
print(prop_df5)

# EI Measure
length(EI_AP_Data$ei_measure)
sum(is.na(EI_AP_Data$ei_measure))
ei_measure_data_o <- EI_AP_Data$ei_measure[!is.na(EI_AP_Data$ei_measure)]
prop_table_o6 <- prop.table(table(ei_measure_data_o)) * 100
prop_table_o6 <- round(prop_table_o6, 2)
prop_df6 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_o6), Percentage = prop_table_o6)
print(prop_df6)

# Achievement Type
length(EI_AP_Data$achievement_type)
sum(is.na(EI_AP_Data$achievement_type))
achieve_type_data_o <- EI_AP_Data$achievement_type[!is.na(EI_AP_Data$achievement_type)]
prop_table_o7 <- prop.table(table(achieve_type_data_o)) * 100
prop_table_o7 <- round(prop_table_o7, 2)
prop_df7 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_o7), Percentage = prop_table_o7)
print(prop_df7)


```

```{r}
# Part 2: Trim Data Part 1-> By education level
# filter df by column value, education level 1 = 2 (graduate)

library(dplyr)
summary(EI_AP_Data)


filtered_mccann1 <-  EI_AP_Data[EI_AP_Data$ed_level1 == 2, ]  
summary(filtered_mccann1)

# 746 available samples at the graduate level

# 2. Summary Statistics
head(filtered_mccann1)

str(filtered_mccann1)

attach(filtered_mccann1)
nrow(filtered_mccann1)
ncol(filtered_mccann1)
summary(filtered_mccann1)

# 3. Variables and Summary of  Variable Statistics
# Study_id: unique id for each study
# Sample_id: unique id for each sample
# es_id: unique id for each effect size
# effect size measure the strength of the relationship between variables or difference between groups
# the larger the effect size the more significance the findings have
# ra value: Pearson correlation: -1 (perfect negative relationship - 1 perfect pos. correlation, close to 0 weak or no relationship)
# primary author
# pub_year: year paper was published
# yi: effect size converted to an r value
# vi: sampling variance for the effect size (SE^2)
# pub_type: type of publication (0- journal article, 1 = dissertation, 2 = conference proceedings
# 3 = unpublished data)
# n = number of participants contributing to the effect size
# ed_level1: level of education at the time of data collection (0 = primary, 1 = secondary, 2 = tertiary, 3 = mixed)
# ed_level2 (academic performance measure): level of education at the time of data collection (0 = primary, 1 = secondary, 2 = tertiary, 3 = mixed)
# country: country where the study took place
# perc_white: % sample categorized as white (USA only)
# age: mean age of the sample
# perc_female: % sample that is female
# ei_construct: (0 = overall ei, 2 = perception, 2 = facilitation, 3 = understanding, 5 = intrapersonal
# 6 = interpersonal, 7 = stress management, 8 = adaptability, 9 = general mood)
# ei_stream (stream or type of EI instrument used): (1 = maximum-performance ability tests, 2 = rating scales based on ability models
# 3 = other broader models of EI that include non-ability constructs)
# EI_measure (EI test used): (1.1 = MSCEIT, 1.2 = MEIS, 1.3: DANVA, 1.4 + STEU, 1.5: STEM
# 2.1 = SUEIT, 2.2: SSRI, 2.3: SREIT, 2.4: TMMS, 2.5 = WLEIS, 3.1 = EQI. 3.2 = TEIQue)
# subject area of academic performance: ( 0 = general, 1 = verbal/ LA, 2 = math, 3 = science, 
# 4 = social studies, 5 = foreign language, 6 = psychology, 7 = medicine, 8 = engineering
# 9 = physical education, 10 = art)
# humanities (science v humanities): (0 = general, 1 = maht and sciences, 2 = humanities and verbal abilities)
# achievement_type: (0 = course gade, 1 = standardized test)

# Publication Year
# Min: 1997, max: 2019, median: 2010, mean: 2009, IQR: 8

#Effect size (r value)
#yi: min: -0.69319, max: 0.71828, median: 0.13076, mean: 0.14799, IQR: 0.69029

# Sample variance
#vi: min: 0.0003938, max: 0.0551461, median: 0.0054171, mean: 0.0079933, IQR: 0.0099334

#Sample Size

# n: min: 18, max: 2195, median: 163, mean: 287.5, IQR: 2177

#Education level
# ed_level1: 2 = graduate

# Academic performance
#ed_level2: min: 1,max: 2, median: 2

#Demographics (repeat after removing NA's)
# Percent white: min: 0, max: 100, median: 52, mean: 56.73, IQR: 78
# Percent female: min: 0, max: 100, median: 66.22, mean: 73.62, IQR: 100
# age: min: 7.68, max: 40, median: 21.33, mean: 22.096, IQR: 32.32



# Proportion tables for Categorical Variables to inform next steps

#country fixed code
# Check total entries and missing values
print(length(filtered_mccann1$country))        # Total length
print(sum(is.na(filtered_mccann1$country)))    # Number of missing values

# Filter non-NA values
country_data_f <- filtered_mccann1$country[!is.na(filtered_mccann1$country)]
print(country_data_f)                          # Non-NA values

# Calculate proportions and create a data frame
prop_table_f1 <- prop.table(table(country_data_f)) * 100
prop_table_f1 <- round(prop_table_f1, 2)
print(prop_table_f1)                           # Proportions table

# Create a data frame
prop_dff1 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_f1), Percentage = as.numeric(prop_table_f1))
print(prop_dff1)                               # Final data frame


# Country
length(filtered_mccann1$country)
sum(is.na(filtered_mccann1$country))
country_data_f <- filtered_mccann1$country[!is.na(filtered_mccann1$country)]
prop_table_f1 <- prop.table(table(country_data_f)) * 100
prop_table_f1 <- round(prop_table_f1, 2)
prop_dff1 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_f1), Percentage = prop_table_f1)
print(prop_dff1)


#Publication Type
length(filtered_mccann1$pub_type)
sum(is.na(filtered_mccann1$pub_type))
pub_type_data_f <- filtered_mccann1$pub_type[!is.na(filtered_mccann1$pub_type)]
prop_table_f2 <- prop.table(table(pub_type_data_f)) * 100
prop_table_f2 <- round(prop_table_f2, 2)  
prop_dff2 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_f2), Percentage = prop_table_f2)
print(prop_dff2)

# Publication Year
length(filtered_mccann1$pub_year)
sum(is.na(filtered_mccann1$pub_year))
pub_year_data_f <- filtered_mccann1$pub_year[!is.na(filtered_mccann1$pub_year)]
prop_table_f3 <- prop.table(table(pub_year_data_f)) * 100
prop_table_f3 <- round(prop_table_f3, 2)  
prop_dff3 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_f3), Percentage = prop_table_f3)
print(prop_dff3)

#Education Level 1
length(filtered_mccann1$ed_level1)
sum(is.na(filtered_mccann1$ed_level1))
ed_level1_data_f <- filtered_mccann1$ed_level1[!is.na(filtered_mccann1$ed_level1)]
prop_table_f4 <- prop.table(table(ed_level1_data_f)) * 100
prop_table_f4 <- round(prop_table_f4, 2)
prop_dff4 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_f4), Percentage = prop_table_f4)
print(prop_dff4)

# EI Stream
length(filtered_mccann1$ei_stream)
sum(is.na(filtered_mccann1$ei_stream))
ei_stream_data_f <- filtered_mccann1$ei_stream[!is.na(filtered_mccann1$ei_stream)]
prop_table_f5 <- prop.table(table(ei_stream_data_f)) * 100
prop_table_f5 <- round(prop_table_f5, 2) 
prop_dff5 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_f5), Percentage = prop_table_f5)
print(prop_dff5)

# EI Measure
length(filtered_mccann1$ei_measure)
sum(is.na(filtered_mccann1$ei_measure))
ei_measure_data_f <- filtered_mccann1$ei_measure[!is.na(filtered_mccann1$ei_measure)]
prop_table_f6 <- prop.table(table(ei_measure_data_f)) * 100
prop_table_f6 <- round(prop_table_f6, 2) 
prop_dff6 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_f6), Percentage = prop_table_f6)
print(prop_dff6)

# Achievement Type
length(filtered_mccann1$achievement_type)
sum(is.na(filtered_mccann1$achievement_type))
achieve_type_data_f <- filtered_mccann1$achievement_type[!is.na(filtered_mccann1$achievement_type)]
prop_table_f7 <- prop.table(table(achieve_type_data_f)) * 100
prop_table_f7 <- round(prop_table_f7, 2) 
prop_dff7 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_f7), Percentage = prop_table_f7)
print(prop_dff7)

```

```{r}
# Part 3: (Filter 2) By EI_Measure/ stream and Descriptive Statistics
# Filter by EI Measure 1.1 and 3.1 

# Filter rows where ei_measure is either 1.1 or 3.1
filtered_mccann2 <- filtered_mccann1[filtered_mccann1$ei_measure == 1.1 | filtered_mccann1$ei_measure == 3.1, ]

# View summary statistics of the filtered data frame
summary(filtered_mccann2)

# Get the number of rows and columns in the filtered data frame
nrow(filtered_mccann2)  # Number of rows
ncol(filtered_mccann2)  # Number of columns

# Proportion tables of important categorical variables to help inform next steps

# EI Measure
length(filtered_mccann2$ei_measure)
sum(is.na(filtered_mccann2$ei_measure))
ei_measure_data_f2 <- filtered_mccann2$ei_measure[!is.na(filtered_mccann2$ei_measure)]
prop_table_f62 <- prop.table(table(ei_measure_data_f2)) * 100
prop_table_f62 <- round(prop_table_f62, 2) 
prop_dff62 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_f62), Percentage = prop_table_f62)
print(prop_dff62)


#Education Level 1
length(filtered_mccann2$ed_level1)
sum(is.na(filtered_mccann2$ed_level1))
ed_level1_data_f2 <- filtered_mccann2$ed_level1[!is.na(filtered_mccann2$ed_level1)]
prop_table_f42 <- prop.table(table(ed_level1_data_f2)) * 100
prop_table_f42 <- round(prop_table_f42, 2)
prop_dff42 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_f42), Percentage = prop_table_f42)
print(prop_dff42)

# EI Stream
length(filtered_mccann2$ei_stream)
sum(is.na(filtered_mccann2$ei_stream))
ei_stream_data_f2 <- filtered_mccann2$ei_stream[!is.na(filtered_mccann1$ei_stream)]
prop_table_f52 <- prop.table(table(ei_stream_data_f2)) * 100
prop_table_f52 <- round(prop_table_f52, 2) 
prop_dff52 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_f52), Percentage = prop_table_f52)
print(prop_dff52)

# Achievement Type
length(filtered_mccann2$achievement_type)
sum(is.na(filtered_mccann2$achievement_type))
achieve_type_data_f2 <- filtered_mccann2$achievement_type[!is.na(filtered_mccann2$achievement_type)]
prop_table_f72 <- prop.table(table(achieve_type_data_f2)) * 100
prop_table_f72 <- round(prop_table_f72, 2) 
prop_dff72 <- data.frame(Category = names(prop_table_f72), Percentage = prop_table_f72)
print(prop_dff72)

# Visualization of outliers for all variables in the `vars` list using boxplots
# Assuming you have a list of variable names in the `vars` list
vars <- c("pub_year", "yi", "vi", "n", "perc_white", "age")

# Check for non-finite values in each variable
summary(filtered_mccann2[, vars])

# Alternatively, check the specific variables for NAs
sapply(filtered_mccann2[, vars], function(x) sum(is.na(x)))
for (var in vars) {
  p <- ggplot(filtered_mccann2, aes(x = 1, y = !!sym(var))) +  # Using 1 for x-axis as a dummy variable
    geom_boxplot(outlier.colour = "blue", outlier.size = 3) +
    labs(title = paste("Boxplot of", var), y = var) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(axis.text.x = element_blank(), axis.ticks.x = element_blank())
  
  # Print the plot
  print(p)
}
```

```{r}
# Part 4: Preliminary Data (filtered McCan 2)


# Group by ei_measure and summarize r^2 statistics
summary_stats <- filtered_mccann2 %>%
  group_by(ei_stream) %>%
  summarize(
    mean_r2 = mean(yi, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_r2 = median(yi, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_r2 = sd(yi, na.rm = TRUE),
    min_r2 = min(yi, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_r2 = max(yi, na.rm = TRUE),
    count = n()
  )

# View the summarized statistics
print(summary_stats)

# Boxplot of mean effect size grouped by ei_measure
y_range <- range(filtered_mccann2$yi, na.rm = TRUE)


boxplot(yi ~ ei_stream, data = filtered_mccann2, 
        main = "Boxplot of Effect Size (r^2) by EI Stream", 
        xlab = "EI Stream", ylab = "Effect Size (r^2)", 
        col = "lightblue", ylim = c(y_range[1], y_range[2] * 1.1)) 
# Barplot of mean r^2 by ei_measure
# Ensures 'summary_stats' is created correctly:
summary_stats <- filtered_mccann2 %>%
  group_by(ei_stream) %>%
  summarize(mean_r2 = mean(yi^2, na.rm = TRUE)  # Calculate mean of r^2 (effect size squared)
  )

# Sort by mean_r2
summary_stats <- summary_stats %>% arrange(desc(mean_r2))

# Checking for NAN values and remove them
any(is.na(summary_stats$mean_r2))  # Checks if there are any NAs
any(is.nan(summary_stats$mean_r2))  # Checks if there are any NaNs
any(is.infinite(summary_stats$mean_r2))  # Checks for infinite values

# QC NaN values
summary_stats_clean <- summary_stats[!is.na(summary_stats$mean_r2) & !is.nan(summary_stats$mean_r2) & !is.infinite(summary_stats$mean_r2), ]


# Check validity of the data
max_value <- max(summary_stats$mean_r2, na.rm = TRUE)
if (is.finite(max_value)) {
  ylim = c(0, max_value * 1.4)
} else {
  ylim = c(0, 1)  # Default fallback
}

# Visualize with a barplot
barplot(
  summary_stats_clean$mean_r2, 
  names.arg = summary_stats_clean$ei_stream,  
  main = "Mean Effect Size (r^2) by EI Stream", 
  xlab = "EI Stream", 
  ylab = "Mean Effect Size (r^2)", 
  col = "orange", 
  las = 2, 
  ylim = c(0, max(summary_stats_clean$mean_r2) * 1.4) 
)


# Save the summarized statistics to a CSV file
write.csv(summary_stats, "grouped_r2_summary.csv", row.names = FALSE)

```

```{r}
# Part 5: Linear Regression Analysis (Ran 6 Models)

# Model 1: Mix of Important Variables
# Fitting the model 
# yi = effect size depndent variable
# vi, n, ei_stream, ei_measure independent variables
model1 <- lm(yi ~ vi + n + ei_stream + ei_measure, data = filtered_mccann2)

# Summary
summary(model1)

# Diagnostic plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model1)

# Save summary
capture.output(summary(model1), file = "model_summary.txt")

# Results

# Residuals
# The differences between the observed and predicted values of the effect size. 

# Spread: range -0.75165 - 0.5241

# Coefficients Table

# Overview of the table
# Estimate: Effect size for each variable. 
# Standard Error: SD of the effect size. 
# t-value: Test statistic for the hypothesis -> H0 = 0
# P-value: measure of significance

# Summary of Coefficients
# Intercept: 0.2170, (p < 0.00) ***
# vi: -6.614, (p = 0.000179), negative relationship
# n: -0.0001465, (p < 0.001), *** weak negative relationship
# ei_stream: 0.01390, (p = 0.195), *** not significant
# ei_measure: not measured -> colinear with other variables

# Summary of Model Fit
# Residual standard error: 0.1918
# Multiple R-squared: 0.04685, 4.69% of the variability in the model
# is explained by this model -> weak explanatory power
# Adjusted R-squared: 0.03992
# F-statistic: 6.766, p = 0.0001833

#-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Linear Regression Analysis

# Model 2: All important variables
# Fitting the model 
# yi = effect size depndent variable
# vi, n, ei_stream, ei_measure, ei_construct independent variables
model2 <- lm(yi ~ vi + n + ei_stream + ei_construct + ei_measure, data = filtered_mccann2)

# Summary
summary(model2)

# Diagnostic plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model2)

# Save summary
capture.output(summary(model2), file = "model2_summary.txt")

# Results

# Residuals
# The differences between the observed and predicted values of the effect size. 

# Spread: range -0.75763 - 0.53177

# Coefficients Table

# Overview of the table
# Estimate: Effect size for each variable. 
# Standard Error: SD of the effect size. 
# t-value: Test statistic for the hypothesis -> H0 = 0
# P-value: measure of significance

# Summary of Coefficients
# Intercept: 0.208, (p < 0.001) ***
# vi: -6.296, (p = 0.00039), *** strong negative relationship
# n: -0.0001465, (p < 0.001), *** weak negative relationship
# ei_stream: 0.007576, (p = 0.56652), not significant
# ei_construct: 0.004743, (p = 0.22096)
# ei_measure: not measured -> co linear with other variables

# Summary of Model Fit
# Residual standard error: 0.1911
# Multiple R-squared: 0.0518, 5.02% of the variability in the model
# is explained by this model -> weak explanatory power
# Adjusted R-squared: 0.04071
# F-statistic: 5.297, p = 0.00003636

#-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Linear Regression Analysis

# Model 3: vi
# Fitting the model 
# yi = effect size dependent variable
# vi independent variable
model3 <- lm(yi ~ vi , data = filtered_mccann2)

# Summary
summary(model3)

# Diagnostic plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model3)

# Save summary
capture.output(summary(model3), file = "model3_summary.txt")

# Results

# Residuals
# The differences between the observed and predicted values of the effect size. 

# Spread: range -0.7339 - 0.5644

# Coefficients Table

# Overview of the table
# Estimate: Effect size for each variable. 
# Standard Error: SD of the effect size. 
# t-value: Test statistic for the hypothesis -> H0 = 0
# P-value: measure of significance

# Summary of Coefficients
# Intercept: 0.17602, (p < 0.00) ***
# vi: -3.01211, (p = 0.0389), * strong negative relationship

# Summary of Model Fit
# Residual standard error: 0.195
# Multiple R-squared: 0.01024, 1.02% of the variability in the model
# is explained by this model -> weak explanatory power
# Adjusted R-squared: 0.007855
# F-statistic: 4.294, p = 0.03887


#-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Linear Regression Analysis

# Model 4: n
# Fitting the model 
# yi = effect size depndent variable
# n independent variable
model4 <- lm(yi ~ n , data = filtered_mccann2)

# Summary
summary(model4)

# Diagnostic plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model4)

# Save summary
capture.output(summary(model4), file = "model4_summary.txt")

# Results

# Residuals
# The differences between the observed and predicted values of the effect size. 

# Spread: range -0.74794 - 0.55261

# Coefficients Table

# Overview of the table
# Estimate: Effect size for each variable. 
# Standard Error: SD of the effect size. 
# t-value: Test statistic for the hypothesis -> H0 = 0
# P-value: measure of significance

# Summary of Coefficients
# Intercept: 0.1686, (p < 0.0) ***
# n: -0.00005, (p = 0.0655), strong negative relationship

# Summary of Model Fit
# Residual standard error: 0.195
# Multiple R-squared: 0.008152, 0.08% of the variability in the model
# is explained by this model -> weak explanatory power
# Adjusted R-squared: 0.005762
# F-statistic: 3.4``, p = 0.06549


#-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Linear Regression Analysis

# Model 5: ei_measure
# Fitting the model 
# yi = effect size depndent variable
#  ei_stream independent variable
model5 <- lm(yi ~ ei_measure , data = filtered_mccann2)

# Summary
summary(model5)

# Diagnostic plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model5)

# Save summary
capture.output(summary(model5), file = "model5_summary.txt")

# Results

# Residuals
# The differences between the observed and predicted values of the effect size. 

# Spread: range -0.73102 - 0.56953

# Coefficients Table

# Overview of the table
# Estimate: Effect size for each variable. 
# Standard Error: SD of the effect size. 
# t-value: Test statistic for the hypothesis -> H0 = 0
# P-value: measure of significance

# Summary of Coefficients
# Intercept: 0.143746, (p < 0.0) ***
# ei_measure: 0.0045525, (p = 0.636), strong negative relationship

# Summary of Model Fit
# Residual standard error: 0.1959
# Multiple R-squared: 0.0005416, 0.05% of the variability in the model
# is explained by this model -> weak explanatory power
# Adjusted R-squared: -0.001867
# F-statistic: 0.2249, p = 0.6356

#-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Linear Regression Analysis

# Model 6: achievement type
# Fitting the model 
# yi = effect size depndent variable
#  ei_stream independent variable
model6 <- lm(yi ~ achievement_type , data = filtered_mccann2)

# Summary
summary(model6)

# Diagnostic plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model6)

# Save summary
capture.output(summary(model6), file = "model6_summary.txt")

# Results

# Residuals
# The differences between the observed and predicted values of the effect size. 

# Spread: range -0.73667 - 0.56967

# Coefficients Table

# Overview of the table
# Estimate: Effect size for each variable. 
# Standard Error: SD of the effect size. 
# t-value: Test statistic for the hypothesis -> H0 = 0
# P-value: measure of significance

# Summary of Coefficients
# Intercept: 0.154395, (p < 0.00) ***
# achievement type: -0.005785, (p = 0.822), strong negative relationship

# Summary of Model Fit
# Residual standard error: 0.196
# Multiple R-squared: 0.0001223, 0.01% of the variability in the model
# is explained by this model -> weak explanatory power
# Adjusted R-squared: -0.002287
# F-statistic: 0.05075, p = 0.8219

#-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Linear Regression Analysis

# Model 7: vi and n
# Fitting the model 
# yi = effect size depndent variable
#  vi and n independent variables
model7 <- lm(yi ~ vi + n , data = filtered_mccann2)

# Summary
summary(model7)

# Diagnostic plots
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model7)

# Save summary
capture.output(summary(model7), file = "model7_summary.txt")

# Results

# Residuals
# The differences between the observed and predicted values of the effect size. 

# Spread: range -0.76164 - 0.53280

# Coefficients Table

# Overview of the table
# Estimate: Effect size for each variable. 
# Standard Error: SD of the effect size. 
# t-value: Test statistic for the hypothesis -> H0 = 0
# P-value: measure of significance

# Summary of Coefficients
# Intercept: -0.2413, (p < 0.0) ***
# vi: -6.77, (p = 0.000121),*** strong negative relationship
# n: -0.0001, (p = 0.00192), *** strong negative relationship

# Summary of Model Fit
# Residual standard error: 0.192
# Multiple R-squared: 0.04297, 4.3% of the variability in the model
# is explained by this model -> weak explanatory power
# Adjusted R-squared: 0.0384
# F-statistic: 9.293, p = 0.000127, ***

```


```{r}

# Part 6:  Decision Tree 

# Install packages 
install.packages("rpart")
install.packages("rpart.plot") 

library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)

# Fitted decision tree with restricted variables (author, sample_id, country, ei_measure, achievement_type)
tree_model <- rpart(yi ~ vi + n + age + ei_construct + ei_measure + achievement_type, 
                    data = filtered_mccann2, 
                    method = "anova")

# Print tree model
print(tree_model)

# Use cp table to prune the tree
printcp(tree_model)

# Prune the tree based on the complexity parameter (cp) value that minimizes cross-validation error
optimal_cp <- tree_model$cptable[which.min(tree_model$cptable[,"xerror"]), "CP"]
pruned_tree <- prune(tree_model, cp = optimal_cp)

# Print the pruned tree
print(pruned_tree)


# Plot cross-validation error 
# Extract and print the cross-validation error for the unpruned tree
cv_error <- tree_model$cptable[, "xerror"]
print(cv_error)

# Find and print the minimum cross-validation error
min_cv_error <- min(cv_error)
cat("Minimum Cross-Validation Error:", min_cv_error, "\n")

# Find the complexity parameter (cp) that corresponds to the minimum cross-validation error
optimal_cp <- tree_model$cptable[which.min(cv_error), "CP"]
cat("Optimal Complexity Parameter (cp):", optimal_cp, "\n")

# Results: 

# 1. Tree Structure
# Node = subset of data
# Splits based on conditions
# * -> terminal node (leaf)

# 2. Nodes
# node -> unique
# split -> condition that splits the data
# n -> # observations in the node
# deviance -> node variation
# yval -> predicted value for observations in the node

# 3. CP Values (Complexity Parameter)
# CP: complexity parameter , low values = more branches, higher values = tree pruned earlier
# nsplit: # branches at each split
# Relative Error: relative error which grows as the tree grows
# xerror: cross-validation error
# xstd: sd of the cross-validation error
# root node: initial split at the root of the tree
# Optimal CP -> minimizes xerror

# Summary of Findings
# 1. Effect size (yi) decreases with increased variance (vi) or larger population sizes (n) 
# 2. Age affected earlier splits, possible significant effect on effect size
# 3. Min CV error: 0.646
# Optimal CP: 0.012

#--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Measurement of Tree Performance


# Cross-validation Model
printcp(pruned_tree)  #  (xerror) for each split

# Root node error = 0.03823
# n = 417

# Add to limitations: #NaN values/ low population after NaN removal
#--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Measurement of Variable Effects

# Which variables are important?
importance <- pruned_tree$variable.importance
importance <- sort(importance, decreasing = TRUE)
print(importance)

# Results from most to least important
# vi = 5.923
# n = 4.438
# age = 2.67
# ei_measure = 0.7698
# ei_construct = 0.4275492
# achievement type = 0.223


# View variable interactions
library(rpart.plot)
rpart.plot(pruned_tree, type = 2, extra = 101, fallen.leaves = TRUE)


```
